title: The Energy You Don't See
partLabel: Part One — Respect the Simple Thing

slides.heading:
The Energy
You Don't See
slides.subtitle: Part One — Respect the Simple Thing

slides.label: Cold Open
slides.text: This is a Tuesday morning. Nothing about it is unusual. A worker picks up a work order — bearing replacement. Twenty-minute job. He's done it a hundred times.

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: He presses the stop button on the conveyor. The belt slows. Stops. He opens the access panel, pulls out a wrench, and starts loosening a bolt on the roller bearing.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.boxText: He pressed the stop button. The belt isn't moving. So he's working.
slides.body: But the electrical disconnect ten feet away has no lock. No tag. Just an unprotected switch that anyone could flip.

slides.heading:
He stopped the machine.
He didn't secure it.
slides.body: There's a difference. And the difference is everything.

slides.heading:
LOTO is just putting
a padlock on a breaker
slides.body: And that's not wrong, exactly. But it's so incomplete that it might as well be. Lockout/tagout isn't a procedure. It's a barrier. The only barrier between you and the energy stored in the machine.

slides.heading:
Most people think
electricity.
slides.body: But OSHA's Control of Hazardous Energy standard recognizes five types. And if you only think about one of them, the other four can kill you while you're busy being careful about electricity.

slides.label: Electrical Energy
slides.boxText: Current flowing through conductors. Touch the wrong thing and current flows through you instead of the wire.
slides.body: But even after you cut the power, capacitors in motors and control boards can store charge and discharge it through you in a fraction of a second.

slides.label: Mechanical Energy
slides.boxText: Moving parts under load. Springs, counterweights, rotating equipment.
slides.body: A spring compressed to 100 pounds of force stays compressed until something releases it. If that something is your hand in the wrong place, the spring doesn't care.

slides.label: Pneumatic Energy
slides.boxText: Compressed air systems. Air tools, cylinders, automated equipment.
slides.body: Compressed air at 90 PSI has enough force to drive a nail through your hand. A cylinder retracting can crush bone. Air doesn't make noise until it moves.

slides.label: Hydraulic Energy
slides.boxText: Fluid under pressure. Lifts, presses, heavy machinery controls.
slides.body: Hydraulic fluid under 3000 PSI can inject through your skin and into your bloodstream. The wound looks minor. The internal damage can be fatal.

slides.label: Stored Energy
slides.boxText: Gravity, elevated components, pressurized systems, chemical reactions.
slides.body: A suspended load doesn't announce itself. A pressurized vessel looks the same full or empty. Stored energy is patient. It waits.

slides.heading:
Energy doesn't care
about your assumptions
slides.body: You can't see most of these energies. You can't hear them. You can't feel them until it's too late. The machine looks safe, sounds safe, seems safe. Right up until it isn't.

slides.heading:
Two minutes
and a padlock
slides.body: That's all that stands between you and forces that can kill you instantly. Two minutes to identify the energy sources. Two minutes to isolate them. Two minutes to verify they're controlled.

slides.label: Back to the Worker
slides.text: The worker from our cold open finishes the bearing replacement. He closes the access panel, collects his tools, and walks away. The disconnect is still unlocked.

slides.heading:
Someone else
flips the switch
slides.body: Maybe they don't see the worker inside. Maybe they assume someone else checked. Maybe they think it's fine because the machine is stopped. It doesn't matter why. What matters is what happens next.

items.text: Five types of hazardous energy: electrical, mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and stored energy
Energy is invisible: you can't see, hear, or feel most energy sources until they hurt you
Stopping ≠ securing: hitting the stop button doesn't control the energy in the system
Two minutes and a padlock: that's all that stands between you and potentially fatal energy
Energy doesn't care: about your experience, your assumptions, or your good intentions

slides.heading: Module 1 — Key Concepts
items: LOTO is a barrier, not just a procedure
Five types of hazardous energy exist in most workplaces
Energy sources are often invisible and silent
Two minutes of proper procedure can save your life

quiz.question: How many types of hazardous energy does OSHA recognize in the Control of Hazardous Energy standard?
options: Four: electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic
Five: electrical, mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and stored energy
Six: electrical, mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, stored, and thermal

quiz.question: Why is stored energy particularly dangerous?
options: It makes loud noises that can damage hearing
It's patient — energy waits until released, often unexpectedly
It dissipates quickly and becomes harmless

quiz.question: What's the difference between stopping a machine and securing it?
options: There is no difference — they mean the same thing
Stopping turns off power; securing involves maintenance
Stopping halts operation; securing controls all energy sources with locks/tags
Stopping is temporary; securing is permanent

quiz.question: According to the module, what stands between you and potentially fatal energy?
